%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Section Objective:** Master the special partial-fraction case where the numerator and denominator have the same degree, \(m = n\).

This section covers a specific improper rational-function case: when the numerator degree equals the denominator degree, \(m = n\). The exam-critical idea is this — the expansion behaves almost exactly like an ordinary proper partial-fraction problem, **except** that an extra constant term appears first.

Many students know how to compute the fraction coefficients correctly but lose marks by forgetting this constant. The constant equals the ratio of leading coefficients and must be written down before any cover-up work begins.

Once the constant is placed, the remaining coefficients \(k_1, k_2, \ldots\) are found using exactly the same cover-up substitution logic as in the proper case. Repeated and quadratic factors follow the same earlier procedures.


## 1. The Rule When m = n

When the numerator and denominator polynomials have the **same degree**, the partial-fraction expansion takes one extra step compared to the proper case.

### THE STRUCTURAL RULE

The expansion begins with a **constant term** \(b_n\) — the ratio of the leading coefficients — followed by the usual partial-fraction terms:

$$F(x) = b_n + \frac{k_1}{x - \lambda_1} + \frac{k_2}{x - \lambda_2} + \cdots$$

This is the **only** structural difference from the proper case.

### HIGH-SCORE DISTINCTION

The cover-up formula for each \(k_r\) is **unchanged** — but it only applies after you have already written down \(b_n\). Students who skip the degree check and jump straight to cover-up will produce the wrong form.

> **Warning:** Writing only partial-fraction terms when \(m = n\) is the single most common mark-losing error in this topic.

#### Note
Repeated roots and irreducible quadratic factors are still handled by the same proper-case procedures introduced earlier.

$$F(x)=\frac{b_nx^n+b_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+b_1x+b_0}{x^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1x+a_0}=b_n+\frac{k_1}{x-\lambda_1}+\frac{k_2}{x-\lambda_2}+\cdots+\frac{k_n}{x-\lambda_n}$$
*When \(m = n\), the partial-fraction expansion starts with the leading-coefficient constant \(b_n\), then continues exactly like the proper case with the familiar simple-root fraction terms.*

$$k_r=\left.(x-\lambda_r)F(x)\right|_{x=\lambda_r}$$
*For distinct linear factors, the cover-up coefficient formula is completely unchanged — the exam trap is forgetting that the full expansion must also include the extra constant term \(b_n\) before these fraction terms.*

## 2. Worked Example and Exam Trap

Consider the rational function:

$$F(x) = \frac{3x^2 + 9x - 20}{(x-2)(x+3)}$$

The numerator has degree 2 and the denominator has degree 2, so \(m = n = 2\). This triggers the special rule.

### STEP 1 — WRITE THE STRUCTURE

The leading coefficient of the numerator is 3 and the denominator is monic (leading coefficient 1), so the constant term is \(b_n = 3\):

$$F(x) = 3 + \frac{k_1}{x-2} + \frac{k_2}{x+3}$$

### STEP 2 — COVER-UP FOR COEFFICIENTS

Apply the standard formula:
- \(k_1 = (x-2)F(x)\big|_{x=2} = \frac{3(4)+18-20}{2+3} = \frac{10}{5} = 2\)
- \(k_2 = (x+3)F(x)\big|_{x=-3} = \frac{3(9)-27-20}{-3-2} = \frac{-20}{-5} = 4\)

### FINAL RESULT

$$F(x) = 3 + \frac{2}{x-2} + \frac{4}{x+3}$$

### HIGH-SCORE NOTE

Strong students identify the constant term \(b_n = 3\) instantly from the leading coefficients **before** touching the cover-up step. This prevents the most common error.

> **Red Flag:** The wrong answer most students give is \(\frac{2}{x-2} + \frac{4}{x+3}\) — which is missing the constant 3 entirely. This answer earns partial or zero credit.

$$\frac{3x^2+9x-20}{(x-2)(x+3)}=3+\frac{2}{x-2}+\frac{4}{x+3}$$
*The final expansion keeps the same simple-root fraction structure as the proper case, but must also include the constant 3 because the original rational function has equal numerator and denominator degree (\(m = n = 2\)).*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- When \(m = n\), the expansion starts with constant \(b_n\) (ratio of leading coefficients) before any fraction terms.
- After writing \(b_n\), find each \(k_r\) using the same cover-up substitution formula as the proper case.
- The most common exam trap: writing only partial-fraction terms and omitting the constant \(b_n\) entirely.

*In the next section we will look at modified partial fractions.*
